meta.title: Cyber Crime Reference Data
meta.description: Combined reference data for all cyber crime categories — includes legal sections, keywords, immediate actions. Cross-questions and evidence checklists are in a separate file.
changes_v2: Fixed duplicate CT019 ID — SIM Swap reassigned to CT018
Removed 52 cross-category duplicate keywords using priority-ownership model
Added category-specific discriminating keywords for all 24 categories
Differentiated IPC sections across previously identical section sets
Added primary_identifiers field per category for high-confidence matching
Added disambiguation_note per category to guide triage decisions
CQ skip-logic keywords cleaned of single-word generic tokens

categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster tricks or manipulates a victim into making unauthorized digital payments or steals money directly from their bank/UPI/wallet accounts through deceptive means such as fake calls, impersonation of bank officials, fake payment requests, or exploitation of digital payment platforms.
keywords: bank wala bana ke call aaya

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for fraudulently using victim's credentials, OTP, or banking identity to access their account

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for impersonating bank officials, RBI officers, or support staff using phone/digital means to extract money

it_act_sections.title: Computer Related Offences
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹5 lakh — for fraudulently accessing victim's bank/payment accounts via digital means

it_act_sections.title: Penalty for Unauthorized Access to Computer System
it_act_sections.penalty: Civil compensation to victim — for unauthorized access to banking computer systems or payment infrastructure

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Disclosure of Information in Breach of Lawful Contract
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹5 lakh — applicable if bank/payment insider leaked victim's financial data to fraudster

ipc_sections.title: Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 7 years + fine — generic online payment fraud

ipc_sections.title: Criminal Breach of Trust
ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 3 years + fine — misappropriation of entrusted money

ipc_sections.title: Cheating by Impersonation
ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 3 years + fine — impersonating bank/official

immediate_actions: TURANT karo: Apne bank ke customer care ko ABHI call karo — transaction freeze ya reversal request karo (number card ke peeche hota hai)
TURANT karo: Net Banking / UPI app mein jaao — apna UPI PIN aur net banking password ABHI change karo
Cyber Helpline 1930 pe call karo — India ka official financial cyber fraud helpline, 24x7 available
cybercrime.gov.in pe online complaint file karo — 'Financial Fraud' section mein jaao
Agar card se fraud hua hai toh card ABHI block karo — bank app ya customer care se
Sabhi transaction IDs, screenshots, call logs save karo — yeh evidence hai, delete mat karo
Agar AnyDesk / TeamViewer install kiya tha toh ABHI uninstall karo aur phone restart karo
Apne email aur banking passwords doosre safe device se change karo
Nearest Cyber Police Station ya Police Station mein written FIR darj karao
Apne bank branch mein jaake written complaint dena — Chargeback / Dispute form fill karo
RBI Banking Ombudsman portal (bankingombudsman.rbi.org.in) pe bhi complaint file kar sakte ho
Apne parivaar aur close contacts ko inform karo — fraudster unhe bhi target kar sakta hai
categories.disambiguation_note: Fallback category for generic online financial fraud. Use only when no specific category (CT002-CT027) matches. Key differentiator: no specific platform/method mentioned.

categories.crime_name: Phishing Attack (Fake Bank / KYC / OTP Scam)
categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster sends fake emails, SMS, WhatsApp messages, or creates fake websites impersonating legitimate banks, government bodies, or payment platforms to trick victims into revealing sensitive credentials such as OTPs, passwords, card numbers, or net banking login details. The goal is to gain unauthorized access to financial accounts.

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for fraudulently stealing and using victim's banking credentials, OTP, or digital identity

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for creating fake bank websites or sending fake communications impersonating legitimate institutions

it_act_sections.title: Computer Related Offences
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹5 lakh — for dishonestly accessing victim's bank account using stolen credentials obtained via phishing

it_act_sections.title: Penalty for Unauthorized Access / Data Theft
it_act_sections.penalty: Civil compensation payable to victim — for unauthorized extraction of banking data or credentials through phishing attack

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Sending Offensive / Deceptive Messages (Reference Only)
it_act_sections.penalty: Note: Section 66A was struck down by Supreme Court in 2015 (Shreya Singhal case) — cited only for historical reference; prosecution now under 66D / IPC

ipc_sections.title: Cheating by Impersonation — via Phishing
ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 3 years + fine — impersonating bank/RBI/govt via phishing

ipc_sections.title: Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 7 years + fine — inducing OTP/credential handover

ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 7 years + fine — creating forged bank login pages

immediate_actions: TURANT karo: Apna net banking password aur UPI PIN ABHI change karo — doosre safe device se
TURANT karo: Apne bank ko call karo — account par suspicious activity flag karo aur card block karwao if needed
Cyber Helpline 1930 pe call karo — financial phishing fraud report karo
cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'Financial Fraud' section select karo
Jo link aaya tha use DOBARA click mat karo — screenshot lo aur evidence preserve karo
Agar fake website pe login kiya tha toh woh account ke sabhi active sessions logout karo
Apna email password bhi change karo — phishing email access ke through email bhi hack ho sakta hai
Google pe 'Report Phishing' search karo — Google Safe Browsing pe fake URL report karo
CERT-In ko phishing website/email report karo: incident@cert-in.org.in
Agar device pe koi app install hua tha toh ABHI uninstall karo aur antivirus scan chalao
Bank branch mein jaake written dispute form submit karo
Apne close contacts ko warn karo — same phishing message unhe bhi bheja gaya ho sakta hai
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to phishing via LINKS (email/SMS). Differentiator: victim clicked a fake bank/KYC link or was called asking for OTP. If UPI app was used → CT004. If email identity was forged → CT010.

categories.description: A cybercrime where fraudsters exploit UPI payment systems and QR codes to trick victims into sending money instead of receiving it, scanning malicious QR codes, approving collect requests, or sharing UPI PINs. Common tactics include fake buyer scams, OLX/Quikr scams, fake payment screenshots, collect request fraud, and QR code payment reversal tricks.

paise aayenge bol ke scan karwaya
payment karne ke baad paise nahi aaye
paise wapas bhejne ke naam pe
qr code scan karke paise gaye
scan kiya toh paise kat gaye

ipc_sections.penalty: Up to 2 years + fine — tampering/forging merchant QR codes

immediate_actions: TURANT karo: Google Pay / PhonePe / Paytm app mein jaao — transaction ke baare mein 'Report a Problem' / 'Dispute' raise karo ABHI
TURANT karo: Apne bank ko call karo — UPI transaction reverse karne ki request karo (2-4 ghante mein karna zaroori hai)
Cyber Helpline 1930 pe call karo — UPI fraud report karo, transaction ID ready rakho
cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'UPI Fraud / Financial Fraud' category
NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India) ko bhi report kar sakte ho: npci.org.in
Agar OLX/Quikr fraud tha toh fraudster ki listing report karo platform pe — account block karne ke liye
Fraudster ka UPI ID, phone number aur sabhi chat screenshots save karo — delete mat karo
UPI app mein jaao — fraudster ke UPI ID ko BLOCK karo future transactions ke liye
Agar QR code physical tha (shop/petrol pump) toh photo lo aur shop owner ko batao
Nearest Cyber Police Station ya Police Station mein FIR darj karao
Apna UPI PIN ABHI change karo — agar kisi ne dekha ya share hua tha
Apne bank branch mein written complaint submit karo chargeback ke liye
primary_identifiers: qr code scan karke paise gaye
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to UPI apps (GPay/PhonePe/Paytm) and QR code scams. Differentiator: transaction via UPI collect request, QR scan, or payment link. If card was used → CT005.

categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster steals, clones, or illegally obtains a victim's debit or credit card information (card number, CVV, expiry date, PIN) and uses it for unauthorized transactions — online or at physical terminals. Methods include card skimming at ATMs, phishing for card details, data breaches, card-not-present fraud on e-commerce sites, and social engineering.

bank ne call kiya card ke liye
naya card aayega bol ke details maange

it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for fraudulently using stolen card credentials (number, CVV, PIN) as another person's digital identity to make payments

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for impersonating bank officials to extract card details or OTPs via phone/digital means

it_act_sections.title: Computer Related Offences

it_act_sections.title: Penalty for Unauthorized Access / Data Theft

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Disclosure of Information in Breach of Lawful Contract

ipc_sections.title: Cheating — Card Fraud

immediate_actions: TURANT karo: Apna card ABHI block karo — bank ka customer care number card ke peeche hota hai, ya banking app mein 'Block Card' option use karo
TURANT karo: Apne bank ko call karo — unauthorized transactions dispute karo aur chargeback request dalo
Cyber Helpline 1930 pe call karo — card fraud report karo, transaction details ready rakho
cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'Financial Fraud' section mein jaao
Net banking login karo — sabhi active card transactions check karo aur koi aur suspicious transaction hai toh note karo
International transactions OFF karo — agar online fraud tha toh card settings mein 'international transactions disable' karo
Online / e-commerce transactions bhi disable karo card pe temporarily
Apna net banking password aur card PIN ABHI change karo
Agar ATM skimming suspect hai — us ATM ke bank branch ko inform karo, RBI ko bhi report kar sakte ho
Bank branch mein jaake written dispute form / chargeback form submit karo
RBI Banking Ombudsman portal pe complaint: bankingombudsman.rbi.org.in
Nearest Police Station ya Cyber Cell mein FIR darj karao
New card issue karne ke liye bank se request karo — ek baar block hone pe same card reactivate mat karo
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to physical card skimming/cloning or card detail theft. Differentiator: ATM/swipe machine involved or card details stolen from physical world.

categories.crime_name: Fake Loan App / Instant Loan Scam
categories.description: A cybercrime where fraudsters operate illegal mobile lending apps or fake websites offering instant personal loans with minimal documentation. Victims are lured with promises of quick approval but are then subjected to excessive interest rates, hidden charges, harassment for repayment even on paid loans, and blackmail using personal data (contacts, photos) accessed by the malicious app. Many such apps operate without RBI authorization.
keywords: loan wapas kar diya phir bhi
loan chukaya par abhi bhi maang rahe
loan mila nahi par fees le li
loan app ne contacts ko message bheja
loan app ne gallery access li
loan processing fee nahi mila loan
loan already paid still harassing app

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for impersonating RBI-registered NBFCs or legitimate lenders through fake loan apps

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Violation of Privacy

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for fraudulently using victim's Aadhaar, PAN, or banking credentials collected via the fake loan app

it_act_sections.title: Penalty for Unauthorized Access / Data Theft

it_act_sections.title: Computer Related Offences
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹5 lakh — for dishonestly accessing and misusing victim's personal data through malicious app

immediate_actions: TURANT karo: Loan app ABHI uninstall karo — pehle saare screenshots le lo evidence ke liye

cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'Cyber Blackmail / Harassment' aur 'Financial Fraud' dono categories fill karo

Google Play Store pe app ko REPORT karo — 'Inappropriate / Financial Scam' select karo

Agar morphed photos bhejne ki dhamki hai — BILKUL PAISE MAT DO, seedha police complaint karo
Apna bank account linked tha toh password aur net banking credentials ABHI change karo
Nearest Cyber Police Station ya Police Station mein FIR darj karao
National Consumer Helpline pe bhi complaint kar sakte ho: 1800-11-4000
Apne aadhaar card ko lock karo UIDAI portal pe (uidai.gov.in) — agar KYC data share hua tha

categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to mobile lending apps with harassment/blackmail. Differentiator: a loan APP was downloaded and it accessed contacts/photos for coercion.

categories.description: A cybercrime where fraudsters lure victims with promises of high returns on investments through fake trading platforms, cryptocurrency schemes, Ponzi schemes, or fraudulent stock/forex trading apps. Victims are convinced to invest increasing amounts of money, shown fake profits on bogus dashboards, and then denied withdrawal. Tactics include pig butchering scams, fake trading groups, impersonation of SEBI/RBI officials, and fake celebrity endorsements.

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for impersonating SEBI officials, RBI representatives, celebrity investors, or legitimate brokers through digital platforms

it_act_sections.title: Punishment for Identity Theft
it_act_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹1 lakh — for stealing victim's financial credentials or crypto wallet keys to misappropriate investment funds

cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'Financial Fraud / Investment Scam' category select karo
SEBI Scores portal pe complaint file karo: scores.sebi.gov.in — agar stock/trading fraud tha

Fraudster ke bank account ki details Cyber Cell ko do — freeze request hogi

Social media pe fraud ke baare mein warn karo (public awareness) — criminals ke profiles report karo
ED (Enforcement Directorate) bhi iss tarah ke large-scale investment frauds investigate karta hai: edIndia.gov.in

categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster gains unauthorized access to a victim's digital accounts — including email, social media, banking, gaming, or any online platform — through techniques like password cracking, credential stuffing, brute force attacks, phishing, session hijacking, or exploitation of weak security settings. Once inside, the attacker may steal data, impersonate the victim, commit fraud, or lock the victim out permanently.

family ko message gaya mere account se

gaming account stolen free fire bgmi

categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster steals or misuses a victim's personal identity documents — particularly Aadhaar, PAN card, passport, voter ID, or driving license — to impersonate them for financial fraud, obtaining loans, opening bank accounts, SIM cards, or committing crimes in the victim's name. This includes unauthorized use of Aadhaar biometrics, AEPS fraud, and identity document forgery.

categories.description: A cybercrime where a fraudster creates fake email addresses, forges email headers, or fabricates entire digital identities to impersonate trusted individuals, organizations, or government bodies. The goal is to deceive recipients into taking fraudulent actions — such as transferring funds, sharing credentials, or signing documents — while believing they are communicating with a legitimate source. Also includes creation of fake profiles, fake company identities, and fake government communications.

categories.description: Cyber bullying and online harassment refer to the deliberate, repeated, and hostile use of digital platforms — including social media, messaging apps, online gaming, email, and forums — to intimidate, threaten, humiliate, embarrass, or emotionally harm an individual. Unlike physical bullying, cyber bullying is pervasive, can occur 24/7, leaves a permanent digital trail, and can involve a wide audience. It encompasses a broad range of behaviors including sending threatening messages, spreading rumors, public shaming, doxxing (publishing private information), exclusion from online groups, morphing and sharing images, sexting under pressure, cyberstalking, impersonation to harm, and coordinated group harassment campaigns. Victims include adults, teenagers, and children — with minors being particularly vulnerable. Perpetrators may be known individuals (classmates, ex-partners, colleagues) or anonymous online actors.

categories.description: Cyber stalking is the use of the internet, electronic communication, social media, GPS tracking, spyware, or any digital technology to repeatedly monitor, follow, contact, threaten, or harass an individual — causing fear, distress, or emotional harm. Unlike cyberbullying which may be public, cyber stalking often involves covert surveillance and obsessive monitoring of a victim's online activity, location, communications, and daily life. It commonly occurs in the context of domestic relationships, failed romances, workplace conflicts, or obsessive fixation on a stranger or celebrity. Cyber stalking may escalate into physical stalking, which makes it a safety-critical offense. It encompasses monitoring social media silently, tracking real-time location via shared apps or hidden spyware, sending repeated unwanted messages after being asked to stop, creating fake accounts to watch the victim, hacking into victim's accounts to read private messages, intercepting emails or calls, contacting the victim's family, friends, or employer, sending unsolicited gifts with tracking capability, and gathering information to enable physical confrontation.

categories.description: Online defamation refers to the publication of false statements of fact about a person through digital platforms — including social media, websites, blogs, forums, review sites, messaging apps, and any internet-based medium — that damages or is intended to damage the reputation, dignity, or social standing of that individual. Under Indian law, defamation can be both a civil wrong (actionable in civil court for damages) and a criminal offence (punishable under IPC Section 499/500). Online defamation is particularly dangerous because digital content spreads rapidly, reaches a massive audience instantly, is difficult to erase completely, and can cause lasting, irreversible damage to personal reputation, career, business, relationships, and mental health. It includes fake reviews, false allegations, fabricated screenshots, impersonation with defamatory content, posting private facts out of context, morphed images with false captions, fake news articles, viral WhatsApp forwards containing lies, defamatory YouTube videos, Instagram posts, Facebook posts, Twitter posts, and coordinated defamation campaigns. The line between opinion/criticism and defamation is legally important — truth is an absolute defense in Indian law, and fair comment on matters of public interest may be protected.

categories.important_legal_note: Online Defamation can be pursued as BOTH a criminal complaint (under IPC 499/500) AND a civil suit (for damages/compensation). Truth is an absolute defense — the statement must be false to constitute defamation. Fair comment on matters of public interest by a journalist or citizen may be protected. Consult an advocate to determine the best course of action. Criminal defamation requires proof of malicious intent.

categories.description: Hate speech and online abuse refer to the use of digital platforms — including social media, messaging apps, websites, forums, livestreams, and any online medium — to promote hatred, violence, discrimination, or hostility against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics such as religion, race, caste, ethnicity, nationality, gender, sexual orientation, language, or place of birth. In India, hate speech is a criminal offence governed by multiple IPC sections, and is also regulated by IT Rules 2021 which mandate platforms to take down hate speech content. Online abuse in this category includes incitement to violence against communities, communal hatred posts, caste-based slurs and abuse, anti-national speech used to target individuals, religious hate content, doxxing of minorities, coordinated harassment of marginalized communities, misogynistic abuse targeting women, LGBTQ+ targeted abuse, regional and linguistic discrimination, and the spread of hateful propaganda and disinformation designed to polarize communities. The challenge in this domain is distinguishing protected free speech and legitimate criticism from unlawful incitement to hate — which requires assessment of context, intent, and likely effect. India's constitutional framework permits reasonable restrictions on free speech in the interests of public order, decency, and morality.

categories.important_legal_note: Hate speech law in India is a sensitive and context-dependent area. Key distinctions: (1) Not all offensive content constitutes criminal hate speech — intent and likely effect matter. (2) Criticism of religious practices, political parties, or government is constitutionally protected subject to reasonable restrictions. (3) The test for Section 153A is whether the content is 'likely to promote enmity' — not merely whether it offends. (4) Section 295A requires 'deliberate and malicious' intent. (5) Platforms are increasingly regulated under IT Rules 2021 to take down hate content proactively.

ipc_sections.title: Promoting Enmity Between Different Groups on Grounds of Religion, Race, Place of Birth, Residence, Language, Caste or Community
ipc_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine. If offence committed at place of worship or in assembly: Imprisonment up to 5 years + fine — PRIMARY SECTION for communal hate speech, caste hate speech, regional hate speech
key_elements: Must promote enmity BETWEEN groups (not just offend one group)
Must be on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, or region
Act must be prejudicial to maintenance of harmony

ipc_sections.title: Imputations, Assertions Prejudicial to National Integration

ipc_sections.applicability: Content that questions loyalty of religious or ethnic groups to India

ipc_sections.title: Deliberate and Malicious Acts Intended to Outrage Religious Feelings
ipc_sections.penalty: Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine — applicable for deliberate, malicious online content insulting religion or religious beliefs
key_elements: Must be DELIBERATE and MALICIOUS
Must be intended to outrage religious feelings
Insult must be to a religion or class of persons
Mere insult is not enough — deliberate malice must be proved
ipc_sections.applicability: Deliberate online content targeting religious sentiments with malicious intent

sc_st_act_sections.title: Intentional Insult or Intimidation of SC/ST Member

not_crime_triggers: Legitimate criticism of a religion's practices or beliefs without inciting violence
Academic discussion or research on communal issues
Satire or parody that is clearly labeled and not inciting violence
Political criticism of a party or ideology without targeting a community for violence
Reporting on factual communal incidents for journalistic purposes
Personal expression of belief or faith that does not demean others

important_distinctions.hate_speech_vs_free_speech: The key test under Indian law is whether the content is 'likely to promote enmity' (153A) or is 'deliberate and malicious' in outraging religious feelings (295A). Mere offensiveness is NOT enough for criminal prosecution.
important_distinctions.online_platforms_duty: Under IT Rules 2021, significant social media intermediaries must proactively identify and remove hateful content. They must appoint a Grievance Officer in India. Victims can escalate to platform's Grievance Officer and then to Grievance Appellate Committee (GAC) if unsatisfied.
important_distinctions.anonymity_challenge: Anonymous hate accounts can be unmasked through court orders directing platforms to disclose identity of account holders. Cyber police have tools to trace such accounts.
important_distinctions.collective_vs_individual: Section 153A protects communities from hate speech. Individual targeting based on community identity can attract both 153A and personal harassment provisions.

This allows the attacker to intercept OTPs, access bank accounts, and commit financial fraud without the victim's knowledge.

categories.description: A malware attack is the deliberate deployment of malicious software — including viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, adware, rootkits, keyloggers, botnets, cryptojackers, wipers, and fileless malware — into a target's computer, mobile device, server, or network without authorization, with the intent to damage, disrupt, steal data, spy, extort money, or gain unauthorized control. In India, malware attacks have grown exponentially targeting individuals, businesses, hospitals, government institutions, banks, and critical infrastructure. Ransomware attacks specifically encrypt victim's files and demand cryptocurrency ransom for decryption keys. They frequently target SMEs, hospitals, and government departments. Malware is typically delivered through phishing emails, malicious attachments, infected USB drives, drive-by downloads on compromised websites, fake app stores, social engineering, and supply chain attacks. The damage from malware attacks includes complete data loss, financial extortion, exposure of sensitive personal and business data, system downtime, reputational harm, and in critical infrastructure attacks — threats to national security and public safety. India's IT Act 2000 and its amendments provide criminal penalties for malware creation, distribution, and deployment, with Section 66F covering the most severe cases of cyber terrorism through malware targeting critical infrastructure.

malware_types.description: Disguises as legitimate software. RAT (Remote Access Trojan) gives attacker full control of victim's device — can see screen, access files, activate webcam/mic, steal passwords.

malware_types.description: Silently records keystrokes, captures screenshots, monitors browsing, steals passwords and financial credentials without victim's knowledge.
malware_types.victim_impact: Password theft, financial fraud, privacy violation

malware_types.description: Specifically targets online banking sessions — intercepts credentials, OTPs, and can perform unauthorized transactions in real time.
Self-replicating malicious code. Virus attaches to files. Worm spreads autonomously across networks without user action.
malware_types.victim_impact: File corruption, network disruption, data loss

malware_types.description: Uses victim's computing resources (CPU/GPU) to mine cryptocurrency for attacker without consent.
malware_types.victim_impact: System slowdown, high electricity bills, hardware damage

malware_types.description: Deep system malware that hides itself from antivirus and OS, gives persistent unauthorized access.

malware_types.description: Injects unwanted ads, redirects browsing, changes browser settings, collects browsing data.

malware_types.description: Designed purely to destroy data — permanently deletes or corrupts files and operating systems.
Enslaves infected computers to perform coordinated attacks (DDoS), send spam, mine crypto, or host illegal content.

43(b): Unauthorized downloading, copying, or extracting data

cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo

Local Cyber Police Station mein FIR darj karao under IT Act 66 / 66F
Agar data breach hua hai involving personal data — Data Protection Board notification required under DPDP Act 2023
Agar business insurance hai — cyber insurance claim ABHI file karo
Do NOT format/wipe device before taking forensic image — evidence destroy ho jaayega
Infected device pe passwords change mat karo — keylogger ho sakta hai — clean device use karo password change ke liye
All other devices on same network scan karo immediately
Lateral movement check karo — malware ne network mein aur kitne systems infect kiye

immediate_0_to_1_hour: Isolate all infected systems from network immediately
Do NOT shutdown systems — memory forensics may be possible while running
Document everything visible on screen before any action
Identify ransomware family using ransom note + file extension
Check nomoreransom.org for free decryptor availability
Alert all stakeholders — IT, management, legal, insurance
first_24_hours: Engage cyber incident response team (CIRT) if available
Identify patient zero — which system was first infected and how
Assess scope — which systems, data, backups are affected

recovery_phase: Restore from clean, verified backup after completely wiping and reinstalling OS

do_not_do: Do NOT pay ransom without law enforcement consultation

categories.description: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack is an attempt to make a computer, server, website, network, or digital service unavailable to its intended users by overwhelming it with illegitimate traffic, requests, or malformed data that exhaust system resources. A Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack scales this by using thousands or millions of compromised computers (a botnet) simultaneously, making it vastly more powerful and harder to block. In India, DoS and DDoS attacks represent a major cybersecurity threat targeting e-commerce platforms, banking systems, government websites, news portals, fintech companies, gaming servers, healthcare institutions, and political/social targets. Attack motivations include financial extortion (pay or we will keep attacking), hacktivism (political protest), competitive sabotage (crippling a business rival's operations), targeted revenge, state-sponsored attacks on critical infrastructure, and as a distraction cover for data theft or other simultaneous cyber attacks. Attack types include volumetric attacks (UDP/ICMP flood, DNS amplification), protocol attacks (SYN flood, Ping of Death, Smurf attack), and application layer attacks (HTTP flood, Slowloris, slow-speed attacks targeting web application logic). Organizations most vulnerable include SMEs without DDoS protection, government websites, banking portals, online retailers during peak seasons, gaming platforms, and media/news organizations. The financial and reputational damage from sustained DDoS attacks can be enormous — including lost revenue, breach of SLA contracts, regulatory scrutiny, and long-term loss of customer trust.

attack_types.description: Flood victim's bandwidth with massive amounts of traffic to saturate network pipe
subtypes: UDP Flood — sends massive UDP packets to random ports
ICMP (Ping) Flood — overwhelms with ping requests
DNS Amplification — uses DNS servers as amplifiers to send large responses to victim
NTP Amplification — abuses Network Time Protocol servers
SSDP Amplification — uses IoT devices as amplifiers
attack_types.impact: Network bandwidth saturation — legitimate users cannot reach service

attack_types.description: Exploit weaknesses in network protocols to exhaust server resources
subtypes: SYN Flood — sends half-open TCP connections exhausting connection table
Ping of Death — sends malformed oversized ping packets
Fragmentation Attack — sends fragmented packets exhausting reassembly buffers

attack_types.description: Target specific web applications — harder to detect as traffic appears legitimate
subtypes: HTTP Flood — massive HTTP GET/POST requests to web server
Slowloris — opens many connections and sends partial HTTP headers slowly
RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet) — slow POST attack keeping connections open
SSL Exhaustion — exhausts TLS handshake processing
DNS Query Flood — floods DNS servers with queries
attack_types.impact: Web application unavailability — consumes CPU, memory, thread pools

attack_motivations: Financial extortion — pay ransom or service stays down
Competitive sabotage — taking down business rival
Distraction — cover for simultaneous data theft or breach
Testing capabilities — criminals testing botnet power
Ideological — targeting organizations based on beliefs

categories.description: A data breach is a security incident in which confidential, sensitive, protected, or personal data is accessed, disclosed, copied, stolen, transmitted, or used by an unauthorized individual, either through external hacking, insider threat, accidental exposure, or negligent data handling practices. In India, data breaches have become increasingly frequent affecting banks, healthcare institutions, government databases, e-commerce platforms, telecom companies, educational institutions, and individual users. Breaches can expose personally identifiable information (PII) including Aadhaar numbers, PAN cards, financial account details, medical records, biometric data, login credentials, and private communications. The consequences of a data breach include financial fraud, identity theft, reputational damage, regulatory fines, legal liability, and in sensitive cases — threats to national security. India's legal framework for data breach response is now significantly strengthened by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act), which mandates breach notification to the Data Protection Board (DPB) and to affected individuals (Data Principals). IT Act Sections 43, 43A, 66, and 72A provide criminal and civil remedies. Unauthorized data access covers both external attacks (hacking, SQL injection, credential stuffing, API abuse, insider theft) and internal data leaks (employee misuse, accidental public exposure, misconfigured cloud storage). Organizations processing large amounts of personal data now have significantly heightened legal obligations under the DPDP Act 2023.

categories.description: Website defacement is a form of cyber attack where an unauthorized attacker modifies the visual appearance, content, or functionality of a website without the owner's permission — typically replacing the homepage with the attacker's message, political propaganda, hacktivist statements, religious content, national flags, or simply demonstrating hacking capability. Website hacking is the broader category encompassing all unauthorized access, modification, exploitation, or control of websites and web applications — including defacement, backdoor installation, data theft, carding infrastructure setup, malware distribution, SEO poisoning, phishing page hosting, cryptojacking, and turning the site into a botnet command-and-control server. In India, website defacement attacks frequently target government websites, political party sites, educational institutions, media portals, financial services, and high-profile corporate websites. Attack methods include SQL injection (exploiting database queries), Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Remote File Inclusion (RFI), Local File Inclusion (LFI), XML External Entity attacks (XXE), Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), insecure file upload exploitation, credential brute-forcing, outdated CMS exploitation (WordPress, Joomla, Drupal), unpatched plugin vulnerabilities, weak FTP/SSH credentials, subdomain takeover, DNS hijacking, and supply chain compromise of web hosting infrastructure. Pakistani hacktivist groups (Team Insane PK, Mysterious Team), Chinese state-sponsored actors, and domestic cybercriminals frequently target Indian websites. Defacement of government websites (NIC-hosted, gov.in domains) is treated with particular seriousness and investigated by CERT-In and cyber police.

categories.description: A personal data leak or privacy violation occurs when an individual's private, sensitive, or personally identifiable information is accessed, disclosed, published, shared, transmitted, or used without their knowledge or consent — by another individual, a company, a platform, a government body, or a malicious actor. Unlike organizational data breaches (CT021), this category focuses on violations from the individual victim's perspective — including sharing of private photos or videos without consent, leaking Aadhaar/PAN/financial details, exposing location data without permission, revealing medical or mental health records, disclosing sexual orientation, sharing private communications, and misuse of data collected through apps, websites, or services. India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act) has fundamentally transformed the privacy landscape, establishing clear rights for individuals (Data Principals) and obligations for organizations (Data Fiduciaries). This includes the right to know what data is collected, the right to correction and erasure, the right to nominate, the right to grievance redressal, and protection from processing without valid consent or legitimate use. The IT Act 2000 provides additional protections through Sections 43A (corporate negligence), 66E (privacy violation — images), 72 (breach of confidentiality), and 72A (contractual breach of privacy). Privacy violations can cause severe psychological harm, relationship damage, professional consequences, safety risks, and financial harm. The overlap between privacy violation and other cybercrimes (stalking, defamation, harassment, sextortion) means many victims need multi-faceted legal remedies.

right_to_privacy_framework.constitutional_basis: The Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21 of the Constitution of India, as unanimously affirmed by the 9-judge bench of the Supreme Court in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) vs. Union of India (2017) — (2017) 10 SCC 1. This ruling established that informational privacy, decisional autonomy, and bodily integrity are all protected under Article 21.

Decisional autonomy — right to make personal choices
Spatial privacy — privacy of home and personal space

right_to_privacy_framework.dpdp_act_overview: Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (No. 22 of 2023) — enacted August 11, 2023 — provides comprehensive framework for processing of digital personal data with recognition of both individual rights and legitimate processing needs

categories.description: Unauthorized data sharing by a company refers to a Data Fiduciary (any organization processing personal data) sharing, selling, disclosing, or transmitting personal data of individuals (Data Principals) to third parties without valid consent, beyond the scope of stated purpose, in violation of their own privacy policy, without legal basis, or in contravention of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 and IT Act provisions. This includes selling user data to advertisers, sharing customer data with business partners without disclosure, disclosing employee data without authorization, sharing sensitive personal data with foreign entities in violation of data localization requirements, data brokers aggregating and selling personal profiles, fintech companies sharing financial data with affiliates, healthcare companies sharing patient data with insurance companies, telecom operators selling call records, and apps sharing behavioral and location data to third-party SDKs without user knowledge. The DPDP Act 2023 has dramatically strengthened protections — requiring consent to be free, specific, informed, unconditional, and unambiguous, with clear affirmative action. Processing beyond consent or without legitimate legal basis now attracts penalties up to ₹250 crore. Organizations must maintain purpose limitation (data only used for stated purpose), data minimization (only collect what is needed), storage limitation (not retain beyond purpose), and must have a lawful basis for every processing activity. The challenge for victims is often not knowing their data is being shared — organizations may hide data sharing in lengthy, opaque privacy policies or use dark patterns to obtain uninformed consent.

dpdp_act_obligations_framework.who_is_a_data_fiduciary: Any person — individual, company, government body, NGO — who alone or in conjunction with others determines the PURPOSE and MEANS of processing personal data. dpdp_act_obligations_framework.what_is_processing: Any wholly or partly automated operation on digital personal data including: collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation, retrieval, use, alignment, combination, indexing, sharing, disclosure by transmission, dissemination, restriction, erasure, or destruction.

Must be UNAMBIGUOUS (clear affirmative action — no pre-ticked boxes)
Can be WITHDRAWN at any time with ease comparable to giving it

categories.description: A fake e-commerce website scam involves fraudsters creating counterfeit online shopping websites that impersonate legitimate retailers, luxury brands, or popular platforms — or setting up entirely fictitious online stores — to deceive consumers into paying for goods that are never delivered, are counterfeit, or are drastically different from what was advertised. These fake websites are designed with professional-looking layouts, fake product images stolen from genuine retailers, fabricated customer reviews, fake trust badges, and attractive heavily discounted prices to lure victims. Common tactics include creating lookalike domains (amazan.in, flipkart-sale.com, myntra-sale.co.in), running Facebook/Instagram/Google ads for non-existent sales, impersonating government surplus or auction websites, creating fake 'going out of business' sales, festival sale scams (Diwali, Holi, Independence Day specials), fake designer/luxury brand websites selling counterfeits at 'discounted' prices, military/defence equipment surplus scams, fake agricultural equipment stores, animal/pet purchase scams, and fake ticket booking platforms. Victims pay via UPI, debit/credit card, or net banking and never receive goods, or receive completely different worthless items. Repeat victimization is common as victims may try to get refunds through fake customer care numbers that extract more money. India's expanding e-commerce market — projected to reach $120 billion by 2026 — and the explosion of social media advertising have made this one of the fastest-growing consumer cybercrimes.

categories.crime_name: Online Job Fraud / Work From Home Scam
categories.description: Online job fraud and work-from-home scams are a rapidly growing category of cyber fraud in India that exploit job seekers' desperation, financial insecurity, and aspirations for flexible income. These scams take many forms — ranging from fake job offers from impersonated companies, advance fee frauds where registration or training fees are collected before disappearing, task-based earning scams where victims earn small amounts initially before being defrauded of large investments, part-time data entry or form-filling scams, fake HR calls collecting personal documents, fake offer letter scams collecting processing fees, fake internship programs, fake freelance marketplace scams, fake YouTube/Instagram like-and-earn schemes, fake crypto trading job scams, and fake call center recruitment scams. The COVID-19 pandemic dramatically accelerated work-from-home fraud as millions of job seekers moved online. Fraudsters specifically target students, housewives, recently unemployed individuals, recent graduates, and people in financial distress. Fake job portals impersonate Naukri.com, LinkedIn, Indeed, Monster.com, and even legitimate companies like TCS, Infosys, Amazon, Google, and various MNCs. The psychological manipulation used includes creating artificial urgency, showing initial small earnings to build trust before demanding larger investments, impersonating HR managers of reputed companies, and exploiting victims' need for income. Task-based scams (YouTube likes, Telegram group tasks, hotel/restaurant ratings) are particularly sophisticated — victims earn and withdraw small amounts initially, are then asked to invest larger amounts for 'premium tasks' or 'VIP membership', and lose everything when they try to withdraw.

categories.description: The Courier/Parcel Scam is a sophisticated cybercrime in which fraudsters impersonate courier companies (FedEx, DHL, Blue Dart, India Post, DTDC), customs officials, narcotics control bureau officers, CBI agents, or police to threaten and extort money from victims. The scam takes two primary forms: (1) The Customs/Narcotics Threat Variant — victim receives a call claiming a parcel containing drugs, contraband, counterfeit currency, or illegal items was dispatched in their name or intercepted at customs. The fraudster impersonating an official demands immediate payment to avoid arrest, FIR, or legal action. This is amplified with 'digital arrest' threats where victims are kept on video call for hours or days while being psychologically manipulated. (2) The Undelivered Parcel/Prize Variant — victim is told a valuable package (prize, inheritance, lottery, gift from abroad) is stuck at customs and requires payment of customs duty, release fee, or taxes for delivery. Both variants use impersonation of law enforcement and government agencies, manufactured urgency, threats of arrest/legal consequences, and high psychological pressure. The 'Digital Arrest' phenomenon specifically became a major concern in India in 2024-2025, where victims are kept on video calls by fraudsters dressed as police/customs officers in fake 'office' settings, terrorizing them for extended periods. Prime Minister Modi specifically addressed digital arrest scams in his Mann Ki Baat address in October 2024. Victims lose anywhere from ₹10,000 to several crores.

special_alert.digital_arrest_warning: THERE IS NO SUCH THING AS 'DIGITAL ARREST' IN INDIAN LAW. No law enforcement agency in India — CBI, NCB, ED, Cyber Police, Customs — will ever arrest someone via video call, phone call, or WhatsApp. Any such claim is ALWAYS a fraud. If you receive such a call, DISCONNECT IMMEDIATELY.
special_alert.pm_statement: Prime Minister Narendra Modi specifically warned about Digital Arrest scams in his Mann Ki Baat address on October 27, 2024, stating that government agencies never threaten citizens via phone or video call.
key_red_flags: Police/CBI/NCB/ED NEVER arrests via phone or video call
Government officials NEVER demand payment to avoid arrest
Customs NEVER calls to say a parcel with drugs was found in your name without physical summons
No legitimate agency will ask you to stay on a call for hours/days
No real officer will ask you to keep the call secret from family
Legitimate courier companies never call for fees to release detained parcels

immediate_actions: DIGITAL ARREST IMMEDIATE ACTION: Agar abhi digital arrest call chal rahi hai — PHONE BAND KARO ABHI. Koi legal agency kabhi video call pe arrest nahi karta. Yeh 100% fraud hai.
TURANT: Family ya trusted person ko inform karo ABHI — akele mat jhoolo is situation mein
TURANT: Bank ko ABHI call karo — recent transactions freeze karne ki request karo
Cyber Helpline 1930 pe call karo — courier scam / digital arrest SPECIFICALLY report karo — 24x7
cybercrime.gov.in pe complaint file karo — 'Courier Scam / Digital Arrest' category
Local Police Station pe ABHI jaao aur FIR file karo — IPC 384 (extortion) + 419 (impersonation)
Caller ke number ko REPORT karo: government portal sanchar.gov.in/TAFCOP pe 'Unsolicited Commercial Communication' report
WhatsApp fraudster number report karo: Settings > Help > Report > Fraud
Agar documents share kiye (Aadhaar/PAN) — UIDAI 1947 call karo aur Aadhaar lock karo
Agar already paise gaye — NPCI portal pe report karo: npci.org.in
CERT-In ko report karo agar large-scale organized operation lagta hai
National Cyber Crime portal pe complaint: cybercrime.gov.in — 'Report Cyber Crime' section
Elderly victim hai toh special attention — elder abuse helpline: 14567 (Elderline)
what_real_agencies_will_never_do: CBI / NCB / ED / Police will NEVER arrest via phone or video call
Customs will NEVER call asking for immediate payment to release a parcel
Government officials will NEVER ask you to keep interaction secret from family
Law enforcement will NEVER accept payment via UPI, crypto, or bank transfer to avoid arrest
No court will issue an arrest warrant communicated via WhatsApp
Legitimate courier companies will NEVER call asking for payment to deliver a prize or inheritance
FedEx / DHL / India Post will NEVER tell you a parcel contains drugs in your name
not_crime_triggers: Genuine customs duty notification sent via official CBIC portal (not phone call)
Actual delayed or undelivered parcel with legitimate courier company — use official tracking
Real legal summons received through official postal channels and properly documented
Legitimate customs agent contacting through official company channels for import clearance
primary_identifiers: cbi ncb ed fake courier arrest
categories.disambiguation_note: Specific to courier parcel + fake arrest threat calls. Differentiator: call mentioned a parcel in victim's name containing illegal items + demanded money to avoid arrest.
